---
title: "Top 5 books I should read according to Claude (seriously)"
description: "I asked the model what I was missing. It actually had answers."
author: "Steve Haigh"
date: "2026-05-27"
categories: [books, llms]
draft: true
---

I asked Claude — the model I've been using as a research assistant through the MRes — for five books I should read, given what it knows about my interests and what I've already read. The reasoning had to be specific; "this is a great book" doesn't earn a slot.

These are its picks. I haven't read them yet. I'll mark each one off as I do.

## 1. *The Major Transitions in Evolution* — John Maynard Smith & Eörs Szathmáry

The rigorous version of what Nick Lane writes about for popular audiences. From self-replicating molecules through eusocial insects, mapped as a sequence of transitions in how information is stored and inherited. Claude flagged this as the academic core under the Lane reading — same questions, more maths, fewer flourishes.

## 2. *The Music of Life* — Denis Noble

A direct challenge to the selfish-gene paradigm, from a systems-biology insider. Argues that the gene-centric view misses how living systems actually work. Claude's note: "He'll sharpen your thinking precisely because he disagrees with books you like."

## 3. *Endless Forms Most Beautiful* — Sean Carroll

Evo-devo, accessibly written. Why a fly, a mouse, and a human use almost the same toolkit genes to build wildly different bodies. Connects the Dawkins replicator-view to the actual mechanics of development.

## 4. *Designing Data-Intensive Applications* — Martin Kleppmann

The software book on the list. Claude's argument was that my sim-infrastructure work — containerised pipelines, exploratory UIs for high-dimensional output — sits squarely in DDIA's territory, and that the book is the most concentrated dose of modern systems-design thinking on the market. Honest caveat: it may already be on my shelf and I forgot.

## 5. *The Eighth Day of Creation* — Horace Freeland Judson

A narrative history of molecular biology's founding decades, from Avery and Pauling through to recombinant DNA. Claude's pitch: "You've read the Mukherjee history of cancer. You're missing the equivalent for molecular biology."

---

*[Open question: how does this list age? Update with one-line verdicts as I read them.]*
